preFix.title: Laying floor tiles in a herringbone pattern
preFix.subtitle: Set rectangular tiles at 45° to create a herringbone layout
preFix.excerpt: How to plan, set out, and lay rectangular ceramic or porcelain floor tiles in a herringbone pattern, including finding the working centre line, mitring end tiles, managing the diagonal layout on a square room, and maintaining consistent joint width throughout.
preFix.sourceNotes: British Ceramic Tile Council guidance. Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling.

content.text: uses more tiles than a straight grid because of the diagonal cuts at the room perimeter, and it takes more time to set out. The payoff is a floor with strong visual depth that makes a narrow hallway read wider. The best tile for herringbone is a rectangle whose length is exactly twice its width: 100 × 200 mm, 150 × 300 mm, or 200 × 400 mm: because the joints then align into a clean zigzag. Tiles that are not a 2:1 ratio create a staggered herringbone with a different character; both are legitimate but they need different set-out.
Step 1, prepare the sub-floor and find the room centre

Check the floor for level and flatness. Correct any deviation greater than 3 mm over 1800 mm with levelling compound before tiling begins. Find the centre of the room by snapping chalk lines between the midpoints of opposite walls. Where the two lines cross is the geometric centre of the room.
Step 2, set the working line at 45°
From the centre point, snap a

at 45° to the walls. To find 45° accurately, measure equal distances along both intersecting chalk lines from the centre point: say, 1000 mm on each: and snap a line between those two points. This line bisects the right angle exactly and runs at 45°. This is the spine that the first row of tiles follows.
Step 3, dry-lay the first tiles to check the pattern

Before mixing any adhesive, dry-lay a run of tiles along the working line to check where the cut tiles fall at the perimeter. In herringbone, the perimeter cuts are always diagonal cuts across the corner of a tile. If the cut at a wall comes out to less than a third of a tile, shift the starting point by half a tile and re-check. Mark the working line position with masking tape along the floor so it is not lost when you start spreading adhesive.

Mix the tile adhesive to a stiff consistency. Working from the centre out, spread adhesive over about 1 m² at a time using a notched trowel. Set the first tile precisely on the working line. The second tile lies perpendicular to the first, butted to the long side, with its corner touching the end of the first tile. The third tile is parallel to the first. Each pair of tiles forms a V shape. Use spacers at the joints: 2 mm is standard for a fine floor; 3 mm for a more rustic feel. Check level across every three to four tiles with the spirit level.

Perimeter tiles need to be cut at 45° to follow the wall line. Mark each cut individually by laying the tile on the laid floor in its finished position and scribing the wall line onto the face with a pencil. Use a wet-cut diamond tile saw for accurate angled cuts; a manual score-and-snap cutter cannot produce reliable 45° cuts on most tiles. Work the perimeter in sections after the main field of tiles has cured.

Allow the adhesive to cure for 24 hours, then grout with an unsanded grout for joints up to 3 mm or a sanded grout for wider joints. Apply with a rubber grout float at 45° to the joints to prevent the float edge from dragging the grout out of the joint. Clean the tile surface with a damp sponge before the grout hazes. Buff with a dry cloth when the grout has skinned.

A double herringbone uses two tiles side by side in each arm of the V. This gives a wider pattern that suits a larger room. A French herringbone (point de Hongrie) uses tiles cut to a parallelogram so the joints form a clean vertical zigzag without offset. It requires factory-cut tiles and is a specialist installation. A running herringbone sets the V pattern parallel to one wall rather than at 45°. This is simpler to set out in a rectangular room and wastes fewer tiles.

attrs.fix: Return to the working line and check with the combination square. If the drift is small, a slightly wider or narrower joint on a few tiles can correct it. If significant, halt, allow the adhesive to cure, lift the drifted section, and relay.
attrs.cause: Working line not accurate at 45°; first tile not set exactly to the line.
attrs.symptom: Pattern drifts off true over the floor

attrs.fix: Prevention: always dry-lay along both working lines to the wall before adhesive goes down and shift the starting point if the perimeter cut is less than a third of a tile wide.
attrs.cause: Starting point not shifted to account for the diagonal approach to the wall.
attrs.symptom: Thin sliver cuts at the wall perimeter

attrs.fix: Use consistent spacers throughout. After pressing each tile, check that it has not been pushed sideways by the adhesive ridge from the previous tile — a slight downward press and twist corrects this.
attrs.cause: Spacers omitted; adhesive ridges pushing tiles out of position.
attrs.symptom: Joints are uneven across the pattern

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
